channel.title: TechTribe Africa
channel.description: Technology intelligence from the continent. The shifts nobody told you about, the markets being quietly built, and the ideas worth understanding before they're obvious.

item.title: Africa didn't adapt WhatsApp for business. Business adapted to WhatsApp.
item.dc:creator: TechTribe Africa
item.description: For the Lagos fashion seller, the Accra clinic receptionist, the Kampala electrician — WhatsApp isn't a channel in their stack. It's the stack. Here's what Western software companies still don't understand, and why African founders are already building the answer.
item.content:encoded:
A Nairobi caterer doesn't have a CRM.

She has a WhatsApp group called "Confirmed Bookings" and another called "Pending." She has a customer who messaged three months ago about a December wedding. She has a starred message she keeps meaning to follow up on.

She's not bad at business. She's running her entire operation on the best tool available to her.

WhatsApp isn't a channel in her stack. It's the stack.

Read the full report →

item.title: The July 2025 pricing switch nobody told SMEs about
item.dc:creator: TechTribe Africa
item.description: Meta changed the economics of WhatsApp automation in July 2025 — switching from per-conversation to per-message billing. Some businesses saw their monthly bills double overnight. Most had no warning. And every guide written before August 2025 is now actively misleading.
item.content:encoded:
In July 2025, Meta quietly switched WhatsApp Business API billing from per-conversation to per-message pricing. The fallout is still hitting businesses today.

Some businesses running volume campaigns saw their monthly costs double overnight. Others who shifted to service-first messaging dropped costs 80%. The On-Premises API was sunset around the same time — which means any WhatsApp API guide from 2023–24 is now actively misleading.

Read the full report →

item.title: What 500 Reddit comments revealed about WhatsApp CRM frustration
item.dc:creator: TechTribe Africa
item.description: We read every thread in r/CRM and r/smallbusiness touching WhatsApp over the last 30 days. The consensus isn't about tools — it's about a pricing wall filtering out an entire tier of business.
item.content:encoded:
82 comments in one thread alone. The top-voted answer on "What's the best CRM for small teams?" wasn't Salesforce or HubSpot — it was "HubSpot Free or Bigin by Zoho paired with WhatsApp Business (no API)".

The API tier is being actively avoided. Not because it doesn't work — because the price of entry has become structurally exclusionary for the businesses that need it most.

Read the full signal →

item.title: Meta's subscription launch just raised the stakes for every WhatsApp-dependent business
item.dc:creator: TechTribe Africa
item.description: May 2026: Meta officially launched paid subscription plans across Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp. For any business that built its customer communication on a platform now actively monetising access, the dependency variable just changed.
item.content:encoded:
On 27 May 2026, Meta launched paid plans across its platforms. The Hacker News thread pulled 290 points and 514 comments — real concern about what this means for businesses that built on WhatsApp as a free channel.

For any in-house CRM play that uses WhatsApp as the messaging layer, platform dependency risk just went up a notch.

Read the full signal →

item.title: The missing middle of African business software
item.dc:creator: TechTribe Africa
item.description: Between spreadsheet and enterprise CRM lies a giant underserved market — spanning CRM, accounting, inventory, HR, and school management. This is the most consequential software gap in Africa right now, and almost nobody is building at the right price point.
item.content:encoded:
Every micro-business in Africa has a spreadsheet. None of them have enterprise software. And the tools priced between those two extremes — built for exactly their scale — essentially don't exist.

This is the missing middle. And it's not one market — it's five: CRM, accounting, inventory, HR, and school management. Each one is a standalone opportunity. Together they're a platform.

Read the full report →

item.title: Competing with forgetting: why most CRMs fail small businesses
item.dc:creator: TechTribe Africa
item.description: Operational memory is becoming a software category. The real reason most CRMs fail small businesses isn't features or price — it's that they're designed around pipelines, not follow-up. Here's the wedge that the best African founders are already exploiting.
item.content:encoded:
Why do most small businesses abandon their CRM within 90 days? Not because it's too expensive. Not because it's too complex. Because it doesn't solve the actual problem: they forget to follow up.

Operational memory — the ability of a business to remember what it promised, to whom, and by when — is the real product. And nobody has built it properly for the sub-$50/month tier.

Read the full opportunity →

item.title: Why African founders understand WhatsApp commerce better than Silicon Valley
item.dc:creator: TechTribe Africa
item.description: DuceCRM, Arkesel, and a wave of African-built tools are solving problems Western tools can't model — because their founders live inside the workflows they're solving. This isn't a distribution advantage. It's an epistemological one.
item.content:encoded:
Silicon Valley builds tools for workflows it understands. African founders are building tools for workflows they live in.

That's not a minor difference. DuceCRM wasn't designed by studying African businesses from the outside — it was designed by founders who are the customer. That's a structural advantage no amount of market research can replicate.

Read the full signal →
